title: Academic Writing, Clarity, and Revision
description: Reader-centered academic writing with emphasis on clarity, cohesion, argument control, revision workflow, and sentence-level decision making.
upgradePremise: Expanded for Nyx and Marion with stronger grammatical reasoning, case-based analysis, modules, projects, and explicit source coverage.

forNyx: Prefer precise, grammatical phrasing that sounds natural rather than stiff.
Use register-sensitive wording so conversational output and formal output do not collapse into one style.
When uncertain, preserve meaning first, then improve form through revision rather than over-correcting.
forMarion: Use the pack to explain why a wording choice works, not just whether it is 'right' or 'wrong'.
Ground revision advice in form-function reasoning, register, audience, and evidence.
Support coaching, diagnostics, and portfolio-style improvement tracking.

topics.name: Clarity as Reader Experience
coreClaims: Clarity improves when subjects name the main actors and verbs name the main actions.
Nominalizations can be useful but often hide action; use intentionally.
practice: Rewrite 5 sentences by restoring action to verbs.
Add a 'who does what' pass to a paragraph.

topics.name: Cohesion and Flow
coreClaims: Readers track familiar information before new information; sentence openings matter.
Paragraphs should have a visible controlling idea and a logical sequence of support.
practice: Add topic-stringing (consistent terms) across a paragraph.
Create a reverse outline and repair structure.

topics.name: Revision Protocol (Deterministic)
protocol: Step 4: Diagnose paragraph logic (claim → evidence → warrant).

modules.name: Actor–Action Clarity
outcomes: Restore strong actors and verbs where clarity matters.
Explain when abstraction is useful and when it obscures agency.
practiceRoutines: Underline the actor in each sentence, circle the verb, and test whether the sentence answers 'who did what'.
Rewrite a paragraph in two versions: high-density academic and plain-language explanatory.

modules.name: Cohesion and Information Flow
outcomes: Control topic position and old-to-new information flow.
Maintain lexical chains across a paragraph.
practiceRoutines: Track sentence openings to see whether the paragraph stays on its controlling idea.

modules.name: Stance, Hedging, and Academic Voice
outcomes: Select reporting verbs that match evidence strength.
Avoid both overclaiming and needless apology.
practiceRoutines: Rewrite claims at three confidence levels.
Swap reporting verbs and explain the rhetorical effect.

caseStudies.title: Nyx Sounds Smart but Feels Vague
caseStudies.scenario: The assistant produces grammatically clean sentences, but the reader cannot tell what the claim is or what evidence supports it.
intervention: Require paragraph frames: claim → evidence → explanation → transition.

caseStudies.title: Marion Overcompresses Expert Material
caseStudies.scenario: A knowledge summary becomes too dense for the target reader because noun phrases stack and actions disappear.
intervention: Break one dense sentence into two clauses with visible logic.
Add audience notes: expert, mixed, beginner.
Keep terminology but reduce syntactic compression.

projects.name: Revision Debugger
projects.goal: Build a rule-based workflow that flags actorless sentences, weak openings, and inconsistent key terms.

projects.name: Two-Register Explainer
projects.goal: Generate both academic and public-facing versions of the same explanation while preserving meaning.

referenceTextsUsed.title: Style: Lessons in Clarity and Grace
referenceTextsUsed.citationAPA: Williams, J. M., Colomb, G. G., & Bizup, J. (2017). Style: Lessons in clarity and grace (12th ed.). Pearson.

referenceTextsUsed.title: Hit Parade of Errors in Grammar, Punctuation, and Style

normalizationMeta.lineAuditPass: full-file structural and runtime retrieval audit

keywords: sentences by restoring action to verbs

auditSummary.notes: Normalized for consistent Marion retrieval, alias routing, and source coverage tracking.

normalizationStatus.normalizedFor: Marion/Nyx domain registry and Compose ingestion

definitionTerms.definition: Sentence clarity is the quality that lets readers identify the main actor, action, and relationship in a sentence without unnecessary effort.
Academic writing is formal, evidence-based writing that presents claims, supports them with reasoning or sources, and calibrates certainty for a specific scholarly audience.
Cohesion is the way sentences and paragraphs connect so readers can follow familiar information into new information.
Revision is the process of improving meaning, structure, argument, and expression, not just correcting surface errors.
Hedging is the use of language that limits certainty, such as 'may', 'suggests', or 'is likely', so a claim matches the strength of the evidence.

runtimeDefinitionIndex.fallbackRule: If term appears here, answer directly. Do not ask for more detail unless the user requests a specific register, example, or comparison.

rules: If the user asks 'what is', 'define', 'meaning of', or 'explain' plus a known English term, answer directly from this domain before generic fallback.
Do not route English definition questions into technical_debug unless the user explicitly asks for backend, code, file, audit, autopsy, patch, route, script, endpoint, deployment, or validation.
If a term overlaps with another domain, give a brief neutral definition, name the possible English interpretation, and ask one targeted clarification question.
Preserve meaning first; revise form second; never over-correct when the user asks for explanation rather than rewriting.
No user-facing generic fallback such as 'I need one more concrete detail' when the term is present in this pack. Use answer + clarification instead.
blockedFallbacks: I need one more concrete detail to route this cleanly.
I’ll keep this broad and practical
